---
title: "Critical Reflection"
---

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../Images/github-pic.png){style="width:100%; border-radius:10px; border:4px solid #3D7A72;"}
:::

::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::::

Entering this module, my experience with web development and publishing tools was minimal. I had some exposure to Python, but tools like Quarto, HTML, CSS, and SCSS were either completely new to me or only vaguely familiar. I also had no real understanding of how to integrate these technologies into a cohesive workflow to build and publish a website, particularly using Visual Studio Code and GitHub. What I did bring, however, was a willingness to experiment and a persistence in figuring things out independently, even when the process felt unclear.

Over the course of the project, I developed a much more practical understanding of how different technologies interact. Learning Quarto was a turning point, as it required me to think not just about writing code, but about structuring content, rendering outputs, and managing configurations. Combining this with HTML for structure and CSS/SCSS for styling made me realise that building a website is not a linear task, but an iterative process of adjustment and refinement. Small changes in styling often had unexpected consequences, which forced me to slow down and become more precise in how I approached debugging.

Working within VS Code and managing files across multiple languages also pushed me to become more organised. At times, I found myself navigating between Python scripts, Quarto documents, and styling files without a clear system, which led to confusion and inefficiency. This experience highlighted the importance of maintaining a clean and logical project structure. Over time, I began to think more deliberately about file organisation and naming conventions, which improved both my workflow and my ability to troubleshoot issues.

One of the more challenging aspects was understanding how to deploy the website through GitHub. While I was able to follow the steps required to publish the site, I did not initially grasp the underlying mechanics of version control and deployment. My commits, particularly early on, lacked clarity and purpose. This reflected a broader issue: I was focused on getting the website to work, rather than documenting the process in a meaningful way. Through this, I realised that version control is not just a technical requirement but a form of communication, both with others and with my future self.

The integration of Python into the website added another layer of complexity. Rather than using Python in isolation, I had to think about how it contributed to the overall presentation and functionality of the site. This shifted my perspective from coding as a standalone task to coding as part of a broader system. It also reinforced the importance of writing clean, readable code, as poorly structured scripts made integration significantly more difficult.

Building and publishing my own website was ultimately the most valuable outcome of this experience. It transformed abstract technical skills into something tangible and visible. Having a live site hosted on GitHub made the learning process feel more purposeful, as the final product could be shared and evaluated beyond the classroom. It also gave me a clearer sense of how these skills translate into real-world applications, particularly in contexts where digital presence and technical communication are important.

Looking ahead, I recognise that my understanding is still developing. While I can now build and deploy a basic website, I am not yet fully confident in my ability to customise it extensively or optimise it for different use cases. However, this experience has given me a foundation to build on. More importantly, it has changed how I approach learning technical skills: I am more comfortable working through uncertainty, more aware of the importance of structure and documentation, and more conscious of the need to balance detail with progress.

Overall, this project was not just about learning new tools, but about learning how to bring them together into a coherent workflow. The ability to integrate, adapt, and continuously refine feels like the most valuable skill I have taken from the experience, and one that will remain relevant as technologies continue to evolve.


[Back to Projects](projects.qmd)
